data.title: 7 Closed-End Fund Buys (And 4 Sells) In The Month Of April 2025
data.description:
April was certainly one of the more exciting months we've had for investing in a while, as the tariffs announced by President Trump kicked in some significant volatility. This was mostly led by the tech-heavy Nasdaq reaching bear territory in a spectacular fashion. Then the S&P 500 hit that 20% decline level ever so briefly on an intraday basis on April 8 from its peak on February 19, 2025. However, as we've entered May already, we have seen the indexes recover everything from the April drop and work back toward their prior peak.

The big drop earlier in April brought about more opportunities, and I ended up being quite active in finally putting some of my cash pile to work. However, I also sold out of several positions, which ended up repositioning my CEF portfolio overall. Some of this was to de-risk, and some was brought about due to corporate action.

Corporate Action Prompts 2 Sells

One of those events was the rights offering being announced for the Cornerstone funds, which I held, Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (CLM). During most ROs, I sell out of the fund upon announcement and then revisit it later. There is often pressure on the share price as it gravitates toward the floor of the offering. In the case of CLM, the floor is a 112% premium. So the offering is guaranteed to be accretive, which is the good news.

Still, it often makes sense to sell the entire position and then revisit after the expiration date. The discount to the current market price isn't often enough when compared to selling the entire position; you can end up with a lower overall cost basis when buying it back after. The last time this occurred successfully for me was selling and then rebuying NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG).

That said, I hesitated this time due to the overall market slump, but I ultimately stuck to the playbook and still sold out of the position. My hesitation would soon prove to be founded because after selling, CLM (along with the market) ended up recovering meaningfully. I sold at a profit and collected many juicy distributions while holding the position, so it wasn't all bad news, but I ended up giving up quite a bit of upside.

In hindsight, when the N-2 filing was posted is when I should have sold the position. That was back on February 21, 2025, and at that time, the premium fell quite dramatically for the fund from ~27.5% to ~17.4%. My rationale for not at that time is that I believed there could be a bounce back in the premium from investors not understanding why it dropped so hard in the first place.

In other corporate action, BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust (BMEZ) conducted its tender offer of 40% of outstanding shares at 99.5% of NAV. As the market was in a slump, this also didn't work out the best. The number of shares tendered was lower than the overall number of shares to be offered for repurchase, meaning that 100% of the position was ultimately purchased.

As we neared the expiration date of the offering, the discount narrowed materially, and that isn't that unusual. The discount then generally widens back out after. However, the offering was during that period of the slump, and then we quickly saw a recovery after. That means the discount did widen, but it wasn't from the share price slumping as we would typically see, but the NAV bouncing back and helping to support the share price. They accepted the shares at $14.2086, and shortly after the results were announced, it was still possible to buy the shares back cheaper. However, the discount level wasn't quite where I wanted to see it drop to, and shares are now ultimately higher.

Overall, despite these two positions being sold out of my portfolio, I still put more net cash to work from my cash pile. So with the other positions I was adding to, I was still participating in the upside elsewhere. These just happened to be corporate actions that hadn't worked out as they usually have done historically, given the substantial amount of volatility and then subsequent recovery. There were also two more positions I sold out of, which we'll discuss below, as they were direct swaps into other positions.

#1 BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund

The first few initial purchases for the month started on April 4th; this wasn't the low of the sell-off, as the following Monday, after the weekend, the market went lower. That said, I picked up BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (CII) initially as the fund's combination of discount and meaningful decline as a more general equity fund made it quite appealing, in my opinion.

The discount for the fund has become more volatile, but it wasn't necessarily the deepest discount that we've had the opportunity to scoop shares up at. Instead, it was closer to the longer-term average that we've seen for this fund. The discount is also notably lower than it was a couple of years ago, when the fund was flirting with trading at a premium on a few occasions.

I recently wrote up a more in-depth update for the fund, but in general, CII invests in an S&P 500-like portfolio and then writes covered calls on its underlying holdings. Writing options along with underlying portfolio appreciation can help to support its relatively higher distribution, which is paid monthly to investors. Late last year, they actually increased the monthly payout amount as well. The latest distribution rate works out to 9.23%.

#2 Calamost Strategic Total Return Fund

Calamost Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ) is another more general market-like fund; however, the fund incorporates a more hybrid approach. That includes investing in sleeves of convertibles and corporate bonds, along with some other smaller allocations of fixed-income instruments. They utilize leverage, which can make the fund more aggressive when compared to CII above.

Given the composition of the fund, it has still delivered similar total returns to the S&P 500 Index over the long term. When compared to the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), as that's the second largest sleeve at ~16.2%, we can see that the fund correlates much more closely with the equity SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) benchmark we are using.

On a total NAV return basis, the results have lagged to some degree, which some investors could use as an argument against owning this fund. After all, the fund is leveraged, so with greater risk, the fund should be seen as outperforming.

Still, I think with the fixed-income sleeve of the portfolio, that would have tempered the overall results. The fund also carries under 24% to the technology sector. SPY has seen its technology sleeve become nearly 32%, so overall, I'd view CSQ as being quite a bit more balanced and adding more diversification.

#3 Reaves Utility Income Trust

Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) is one of the more popular CEFs due to their long history of never having cut their regular monthly distribution. In fact, they've grown it over time and have an inception prior to the Global Financial Crisis. That's only something that a handful of funds can boast about.

This one was one of my larger positions at one point, but over time, it slowly slid to a lower overall allocation due to adding to other funds in my portfolio instead.

UTG has regularly traded near parity with its NAV per share, not allowing for a real 'bargain' to pick up shares. However, with this latest downturn, it did provide an overall slump that, I felt, was worthwhile to pick up shares—even if the discount wasn't massive.

One of the reasons that UTG was hit particularly hard during this latest market downturn is that its top holdings have started to include some really volatile and high-flying utility names. Talen Energy (TLN), Vistra (VST), and Constellation Energy (CEG) are names that are associated with the AI revolution and have become quite richly priced in anticipation of significant earnings growth on the back of that.

These names took a relatively harder hit compared to the broader Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU). Though TLN wasn't too far off, VST and CEG saw significant declines.

#4 XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust And #5 Eagle Point Income Company

I'm lumping both XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT) and Eagle Point Income Company (EIC) into the same discussion because it was the direct result of selling out of Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC) and Oxford Lane Capital Corp (OXLC). ECC and OXLC are the two other sold positions this month, but those proceeds went into XFLT and EIC. More went into EIC for me, as I had already been adding quite aggressively to XFLT after their distribution cut.

The idea here was presented by Stanford Chemist, which prompted me to participate along with the move. The idea was that OXLC and ECC were selling off quite hard but were otherwise holding up relatively well compared to what may happen when the estimated monthly NAVs were updated. There was some sell-off when ECC announced their updated NAV, but this was another case where the overall recovery in the market meant that it wasn't the most successful swap trade.

At the same time, I am still comfortable with making this move as it also de-risked my portfolio at a time when I was increasing risk by drawing down my cash balance.

Since OXLC and ECC are CLO equity-focused, moving to XFLT and EIC presents more limited risk on a relative basis. This is because EIC focuses on CLO debt rather than equity. XFLT is a hybrid alternative fund that focuses on CLO equity and debt, as well as investing in individual senior loans.

I had been considering making this move myself anyway for a while, as ECC and OXLC have been on a strong run for several years now. When volatility increases during economic downturns, these funds get hit quite hard due to their added leverage on an already volatile asset. So it's a bit like musical chairs, you need to be prepared and try not to be the last one standing.

Before getting back into ECC or OXLC, I'll likely be waiting for when the distribution cuts. With these funds, it is generally a matter of when and not if, due to their volatile nature. That said, that "when" may be in a few months or in a few years. Though investors likely take comfort in knowing that OXLC has declared its same monthly distribution out through September.

Another area that I find interesting and believe income-focused investors could find appealing is the preferred and baby bonds from the CLO funds themselves. I know they certainly aren't as exciting as investing in the common, but they come with much lower risk as well. Currently, I continue to hold Oxford Lane Capital 6.25% Preferred (OXLCP) and XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust 6.5% Series A (XFLT.PR.A).

#6 Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (EVT), I ended up investing in on April 9th; this is a name I also added back in January of this year. Though that batch of shares is in the red at this time, the latest round picked up for April is in the green. This is another fund that I wrote about quite recently, with one of its notable features being that it is a more value-oriented portfolio.

In general, the fund still saw a slump overall with the broader market, so that wasn't entirely negated, but the declines were slightly reduced. Further, the fund's discount in the last couple of years has remained quite deep, and that made it another attractive option to add to. The fund was even flirting with a double-digit discount level during April at one point. Similar to what we saw for CII above, this fund also was enjoying some premium previously at one point, but has slipped to a deeper discount over the last couple of years.

One of the reasons for this could be from more competition in the ETF space, adding additional methods for investors to put capital to work in income-focused funds. Since EVT is leveraged, its borrowing costs also increased when the Fed aggressively raised rates, making leveraged funds less appealing. Higher rates also had another impact, and that could have impacted CII as well, because there were more alternatives in less risky assets in terms of looking for income.

Finally, EVT also cut its distribution in 2022, along with most of the other Eaton Vance equity funds. That seems likely to be one of the more impactful factors in seeing the fund's discount remain wide, in my opinion. They had since raised it, but CII never cut during that period, and also raised their payout more recently as well.

#7 Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund

I added to my Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund (ETG) position on April 16th, and this was the last name I added to my CEF portfolio for April. So the second half of the month was really quiet for me. At this point, I had already put a good portion of my cash pile to work, and the market was already starting to recover meaningfully. I was adding to individual dividend-paying names during this time as well in my other portfolios, so cash was getting put to work elsewhere, too.

ETG brings a bit of a different approach with the inclusion of some global tilt, with foreign equities accounting for 47% of the fund's investment mix as of the end of March 31, 2025.

Another characteristic of the fund is that it tends to skew toward financials over the tech sector. That can often make it more similar to EVT, which we touched on above.

That said, its sector allocation can tend to see some meaningful fluctuations. In our last full update, we looked at the fund's sector allocation as of the end of October 31, 2024. Financials were the largest allocation but came in at 23.4% compared to 22.5% for technology. The weighting above now as of March 31, 2025 firmly overweight the financial sector. So that is something to keep an eye on when holding this fund.

Similar to EVT above as well, the fund saw a reduction in its monthly distribution in 2022, only for it to be increased once again. Perhaps unsurprisingly, with that move, the fund also saw a move that took it from trading at a premium to a discount in a rather rapid manner.

Interestingly, the fund's discount has been narrowing more recently rather than widening out as we often see when more volatility is around. Prompting me to add to ETG wasn't the discount alone, but the discount combined with the overall decline, bringing pressure to its share price. One of the reasons we could be seeing the discount narrow is that global equities have been outperforming U.S. equities this year. This is partially driven by the tariffs that are expected to hurt the U.S. the most, and due to valuation differences. U.S. equities headed into this year at a stretched valuation, while international markets looked like relative bargains.

data.title: Tariffs And Inflation: Looking For Signs Of Impact
data.description:
Originally published on May 13, 2025

U.S. consumer prices declined in April, but the full impact of tariffs may not be showing yet. Oscar Munoz, Chief U.S. Macro Strategist, TD Securities joins MoneyTalk to discuss what this means for interest rates and markets.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Well, US inflation has cooled to its lowest level since 2021. But are the effects of trade tariffs beginning to be felt yet? Joining us now to discuss is Oscar Munoz, Chief US Macro Strategist with TD Securities.

Oscar, thanks for joining the show.

Oscar Munoz: Thank you, Anthony. Thanks for the invitation.

Anthony Okolie: All right. So Oscar, we've got the latest US inflation report. What's your headline reaction?

Oscar Munoz: Look, I think that this is the calm before the storm. That is the way we would characterize today's report. We were on the right side of consensus this morning, looking for 0.2% month-to-month increases for both the core and the headline. We were assuming some services disinflation, particularly when looking into OER, airfares, and hotel rates.

But the tariff pass-through is becoming more visible if you look at the details of the report. So I think the main message for today is that prices or inflation is steady for now. But inflation is likely to accelerate in the coming months.

Anthony Okolie: OK, so what stood out for you in the report? And are we starting to see any signs of the impact of the tariffs yet in the numbers?

Oscar Munoz: Yes, actually. I think, as I mentioned before, if you look at the details, particularly some goods items, you're seeing some pass-through, at least, some strength in some of those categories. You can think about household goods, recreation and communication goods. Those sectors are more exposed to Chinese imports. You're starting to see some signs there.

If you look at core goods excluding vehicles inflation, that's been gaining momentum since the start of the year on a month-on-month basis. I think that's probably going to continue over the next few months, thinking between now and the summer report.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So inflation, as you say, could accelerate starting with the data for May. What are you expecting?

Oscar Munoz: Well, it's a little bit early. We still need to look to the forecast for that month after the data that we received today. But I wouldn't be surprised to see-- if you look at the average of core CPI inflation over the last three months, it's been roughly 0.2% month on month. I wouldn't be surprised to see the May print to move higher into the 0.3% to 0.4% month-on-month area.

We do still expect a larger acceleration in the June and July reports, because that's where we see most of the pass-through, the bulk of it actually translating into consumer price inflation. So again, I think the main take from today is that we're seeing some early signs. But strength is likely to accelerate over coming reports.

Anthony Okolie: OK. So, big question is, what does this all mean for the Fed? What's your interest rate outlook going forward?

Oscar Munoz: Look, I think given the recent announcement regarding reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods, we think that really reduces the left tail risk of a major deceleration in economic activity. So, recession odds really have to drop. This means that if the labor economic activity side is able to remain resilient to the US consumer, it means that the Fed can continue focusing on inflation outcomes as we go through this tariff pass-through.

It also means that maybe the economy does not need monetary policy support in the near term. We do think that probably the Fed will ease at some point this year, but probably toward the back half of 2025.

Anthony Okolie: And my final question, you talked a little bit about the economy. What is your thoughts about the general health of the US economy? And what's your take going forward?

Oscar Munoz: Yeah, look, I think the partial lifting of trade uncertainty, the trade détente between China and the US, means that planning and spending can recover in the near term. Consumer and business expectations and sentiment should improve. I think we're seeing that in equity prices yesterday and today. Financial conditions should support the economy in the near term.

So I'm looking for steel frame economic activity data over the next few months, particularly on the spending and labor market conditions. I think Q2 data should be OK. Q3 is where there is some question mark, given that, as I mentioned before, you're going to see the bulk of the impact from high inflation there. And we have to see how the consumer reacts to that in terms of outlays, in terms of spending, given that inflation is going to lower real incomes.

data.title: Chimera Investment Corporation NT 29: High Yield And Attractively Priced
data.description:
I believe that Chimera’s 9.25% Chimera Investment Corporation NT 29 (NYSE:CIMO) notes are attractive to the market, trading just below par despite an investment-grade issuer, covenant-lite structure, and proven balance-sheet agility, which creates a rare Strong Buy opportunity for high-income investors. The CIMO are 9.25% senior direct unsecured notes due on August 15, 2029, and issued by mortgage real estate investment trust (mREIT), Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM). With the firm increasing its profitability at a time when it has a fairly attractive debt profile and the notes available below par, this is a good time to invest in them.

Background and Terms

Chimera Investment Corporation is largely focused on investing in a diversified portfolio of mortgage assets, including residential mortgage loans and mortgage-backed securities. It seeks to earn attractive risk-adjusted returns for its shareholders through net interest income and asset management services. The company has a long history of operating in the mortgage market and has a demonstrable ability to navigate various economic cycles. Its December 2024 acquisition of Palisades Group, a residential real estate credit manager that used cheap local movers with trucks to service Florida, further expands Chimera's capabilities and is expected to contribute to future earnings growth. In order to fund its investments in mortgage assets such as purchases of Agency and Non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), and for other purposes, the company sought $74.75 million from the market, which it obtained by issuing these notes. When I say “the market”, what is really meant is that Morgan Stanley (MS), RBC Capital Markets, UBS Securities, Wells Fargo Securities, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods, Inc., and Piper Sandler Companies (PIPR), who served as the offering’s book-running managers, bought the notes at a discount. The notes have an investment-grade rating of BBB from Egan-Jones Ratings Company. They have been trading since August 22, 2024. They are the second such notes issued by the company in 2024, after the $65 million CIMN, for a total unsecured debt raise in 2024 of $140 million. Phillip J. Kardis II, the President and CEO, remarked after the closure of the CIMN raise that, “We believe the ability to grow our business through successful capital raising activities will benefit our shareholders over the long term.”

The notes pay 9.25% per year, with payments made every quarter, on February 15, May 15, August 15, and November 15 of each year. The notes are redeemable at the issuer's option on or after August 15, 2026, at a price of $25 per note plus accrued and unpaid interest. Small-denomination notes with a par value of less than $1,000 are known as baby bonds and are attractive to retail investors because of their affordability and attractive yield. At 9.25%, the notes are more attractive in terms of yield compared to 10-year U.S. Treasuries, which currently offer a 4.37% yield. This is an attractive level of compensation for the risk level, even with the prospect of rising interest rates. The 4.88% spread between Chimera’s bond yield and the benchmark is one of the widest compensation levels I have seen outside of fallen angels or stressed credit names. This is fair compensation for moderate credit risk.

Chimera has a call option beginning August 15, 2026, at par which, provides a hard floor at $25, protecting investors from downside if market yields compress. If the company should decide to refinance at lower rates or retire high‑cost debt, note holders realize full principal plus accrued interest.

Chimera’s Debt Profile

As of March 31, 2025, Chimera has $10.4 billion in total debt, excluding any payables, of which $7.3 billion is securitized debt at fair value, and nearly $3 billion in secured financing agreements. Under the notes’ indenture, there are no limits to how much debt that Chimera can assume, nor will the company accept any financial covenants, such as covenants restricting dividend payments or governing share repurchases. Covenant-lite structures usually demand extra yield. The absence of them gives Chimera the freedom to optimize capital allocation into high‑return mortgage assets and opportunistic investments without risk of technical default. The Egan-Jones BBB rating indicates that Chimera has a “moderate level of creditworthiness with moderate sensitivity to evolving credit conditions.” The combination of moderate creditworthiness and absence of restrictive covenants underpins my view that Chimera can both grow its core earnings and meet its obligations.

My own examination of the company’s debt profile shows that it is fairly attractive, showing that Chimera has comfortable buffers for coupon payments, even under stressed spread scenarios.

The issuer’s call option beginning August 15, 2026, at par provides a hard floor at $25, insulating investors from downside if market yields compress. Should Chimera refinance at lower rates or retire high‑cost debt, note holders realize full principal plus accrued interest.

Chimera is Growing Stronger

Investing in such a note carries credit risk, the risk that Chimera will not be able to repay its obligations. It is important to note, that the notes carry, as we said, a BBB investment grade rating. Furthermore, in the last five years, Chimera has grown net income available to common shareholders from $15.1 million in 2020 to $125.25 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM). In the Q1 2025 earnings call, Phil Kardis notes that, “Earnings available for distribution improved by 11%, our book value increased by 7.4%, and our economic return was 9.2%”, going on to suggest that the Palisades acquisition had been successfully and quickly integrated. With improving profitability comes decreasing risk with respect to the notes. This is because greater profits mean that Chimera’s capacity to meet its obligations rises. By way of example, Kadis explained that,

“This quarter, we exercised our call rights on all our non-Remic securitizations and issued 2 new securitizations backed by those loans. This was effectively a cashout refinancing that unlocked $187 million at a reinvestment hurdle below 6%, and there's more.”

This demonstrates management’s proactive balance sheet management, driving down future funding costs and preserving excess liquidity. The company’s proactive strategy enhances Chimera’s ability to service the CIMO notes even under widening spreads. Although Chimera’s debt structure includes both recourse and non-recourse leverage, they are both fairly low and manageable, with GAAP recourse leverage at 1.2:1 and total GAAP leverage at 3.09:1 as of Q1 2025.

Another risk is that interest rates rise such that they put pressure on Chimera's profitability. At present, there is no indication of aggressive interest rate hikes, and this is unlikely given the economic certainties and mix of inflationary pressures and recession risks. Market volatility in the broader financial markets could also impact the trading price of CIMO. However, as a senior unsecured debt, CIMO's price is generally less volatile than the underlying common stock. Since its issuance in 2024, the price has been fairly stable.

The Right Time to Buy

At $24.97, the notes are trading slightly below par value, giving investors free optionality should yield compress or Chimera call them at $25 in August 2026. I believe that this is the right time to buy, as the investor can maximize yield and minimize potential capital loss if the notes are called in 2026. Investors who buy at above par run the risk that if the notes are called in August 2026, they would face a capital loss. Buying at above par may be acceptable if investors are willing to take on that risk.

Risks

Before investors take the leap, investors need to weigh various company and bond-specific risks. First, if a sustained uptick in mortgage spreads occurs or there is Fed policy tightening, this could compress Chimera’s net interest spread, pressuring earnings available for distribution. Second, a sudden contraction in the non-Agency RMBS or securitized loan markets could widen Chimera’s asset-liability spread beyond what its hedging strategy can cover, risking NAV drawdowns and rating pressure. In addition, although these baby bonds are more liquid than most securitizations, a spike in credit-spread volatility or a market-wide sell-off could widen bid-ask spreads, delaying execution or forcing steep markdowns. In the Q2 1025 earnings call, management addressed this, with CIO Jack McDowell assuring analysts that, "We have built additional liquidity and positioned our portfolio to withstand volatility, spread widening, and funding shocks if they were to emerge."

Conclusion

Chimera’s CIMO notes offer an attractive, high single-digit yield, have an investment grade rating, a call-at-par floor, and a sub-par price offering optionality. Given, the firm is growing more profitable and will therefore be more able to meet its obligations, this is happening as the company already has an attractive debt profile. I strongly recommend buying these notes.
